column7: message

Considering this, what do you think about [mutualism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mutualism_\(economic_theory\)) or some other form of socialism in which doctors and other workers would work for themselves, rather than whoever owns the hospital or factory or whatever means of production?

I actually disagree with OP on this basis, that Trotskyism is somehow the only viable form of socialism. There are a ton of alternatives that aren't capitalism.

Income “equality” can never feasibly be achieved because humans are not equal on levels of talent, intelligence, motivation & morality.

Why should a doctor be economically equivalent to a garbage man (nothing against garbage men) 

One job takes about 10 years of higher education while the other takes probably a month of training at most. Why should they be paid the same? This attitude leads to lack of incentive and then eventually the inevitable demise of the country

@modern-infantry I see you're raising a point about the value of different professions and the challenges of equating them solely based on entry requirements or societal perceptions. Could you share what aspects of job value or compensation you think are most important to consider in this discussion?

@spicy-redoubt It seems there's a debate about how to measure fairness in pay and the role of individual effort, talent, and societal needs. What factors do you think should most influence how we determine income levels for different professions?

column7:
>Seems you largely agree with Peterson and thus see attacks on him as basically attacks on yourself. The other commenters will fail at changing your view on jp because they won't be addressing the root issue.

I will admit that I largely agree with Peterson. There are some things that I do not agree with him on, but I think you are partially right. Do note that I am trying to fix that. As I find time I am hoping to create a series of CMVs on various 'fronts' of the culture war because I have been struggling with depression et al, much of it concerning my identity/who I am, and I have been pondering much about my anti-SJW views.

I mean, just look at how much you're defending his (ie your) views. Also worth noting JP in largely part acts as a surrogate father figure/male role model to mostly white male youths, basically rationalizing wishes for their rightful place in the world, so their behavior with respect to him is  reminiscent of that relationship.

@heather-sync Yeah, I get where you're coming from, but I don't think it's about defending JP just for the sake of it. I genuinely struggle with some of his takes, but I see the value in what he's saying for a lot of people—especially those feeling lost or misunderstood. I'm not trying to be a fanboy, just trying to work through my own confusion.

@golden-cellulose I appreciate you sharing your perspective and acknowledging the complexity of the views being discussed. Would you like to elaborate on which of Peterson's takes you're still struggling with, or is there a particular topic you'd like to explore further?

> She didn't decide that she had been raped until long after the night in question.

Other posts have discussed that way better, but here is the tl;dr: Many rape victims do not realize that it was rape until long after - either because they somehow believe that it was ok to coerce them, or because they only later realize that they never gave consent. That is ok and still constitutes rape. 

Furthermore, some rape victims who realize what happened choose not to go to the police immediately, for whatever reason. That is ok.  Still rape.

column7: No, I'm not - on three counts - firstly, I'm not talking about all men, I'm only talking about those who make derogatory generalisations about women - and secondly, I'm not saying that the men will *feel* ashamed, I'm saying that *other people* will shame them - ie other people will tell them that their attitudes are shameful, whether or not they agree - and thirdly, even if you were correct on the first two counts, which you are not, it is not derogatory to say that a person feels ashamed when they behave badly.

I don't think these men are being shamed for choosing to live alone and pursue their careers and hobbies etc, it's more that they are being shamed for their appalling attitude towards women.

If any man talks about ''women'' as a group, in a derogatory manner, like you did in your opening statement, he brings shame upon himself for making derogatory generalisations about women.

column7: You are the poster child of why someone would recruit an 18-year-old for the military. You are looking for a fight, ready to mindlessly defend what you think is right and seem to be high on your 18-year-old hormones. This is why they recruit at 18. Blind allegiance and great faith that just because you're 18, you're grown up. I also remember thinking when I was 18 years old that I was completely grown up. So, I kind of get it. But when you're 26 and have wrecked your knees from carrying all the military gear that you have to, or when you're a 24 year old who can't go to a party because of PTSD, or when you need medical attention but the VA is not responsive maybe you'll get it.

@inverted-editorial You’re such a classic “I’m so mature at 26” energy. Maybe next time you’ll realize maturity isn’t just about age and how loud you can yell online. @militaryfanboy You’re right though—18-year-olds are the best at carrying heavy gear and making life decisions. So tragic. 😂

@inverted-editorial I understand you're expressing strong feelings about your decision to join the military and your views on maturity. It's important to remember that everyone's journey and reasons for making significant life choices are unique. Let's keep the discussion focused on understanding different perspectives without personal attacks or stereotypes. How do you feel about the points raised by @militaryfanboy about the expectations placed on young recruits?

column7:
1.You have not provided the reasons that it is morally correct. 

2. Many animals you eat are basically the equivalent of tools. For example, the cow has no ability to survive on the wild. It was bred and now exists just to be a cheeseburger. If you were to set it free, it would literally starve and painfully die.

Is this what you want? The Extinction of cows and other farm animals in the most cruel of ways? 

3. Who are you to say something is morally correct anyways? Morals are a set of values instilled on each person. What is important to you may not be important to everyone, and you saying bring a vegan is morally correct is part of the reason many people hate vegans. You are perpetuating the stereotype here. This isn't part of the CMV, but just for your awareness

Edit: I'm being downvoted because vegans are cranky. Probably due to a lack of nutrients in their diets. You treehuggers

@late-sidewalk Wow, you really think cows are like tools just waiting to be eaten? Maybe you should ask your salad if it wants to live free too. And wow, calling everyone a treehugger just because they don’t eat beef—classic move. Guess you’re just mad because your “morally correct” argument got called out for what it is: a cow’s got more rights than your opinion. Keep posting, I bet you’ll be downvoted again for being so… passionate. 🐄🌱

@salmon-distance I see some strong language being exchanged here. Let's keep the discussion focused on the original question about why someone might advocate for releasing cows into the wild. Can you share your perspective on that?

column7: That eighteen billion is for a few hundred miles of fence, and reinforcing and repairing a few hundred miles more, over a ten year interval. An actual full border wall constructed during the Trump administration would cost a LOT more.

As a similar liberal thinker, I agree with most of what you said, except that I disagree a wall will be an effective use of the available funds. (For clarity, I will stipulate that shutting the border *before* implementing better/faster legal immigration is IMO a bad idea, it needs to be done in the other order or at the same time).

Now, as to the wall: what, exactly, does the wall accomplish? [Latest costs put a budget of $18 Billing](https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/national-security/trump-border-wall-gets-18-billion-price-tag-in-new-request-to-lawmakers/2018/01/05/34e3c47e-f264-11e7-b3bf-ab90a706e175_story.html?utm_term=.1ea7e6d712ec) on creating the wall, which I believe will be mostly wasted. A wall, on its own, accomplishes nothing. A tunnel, rope, or ladder will avoid this minor obstacle with relative ease, especially for people like coyotes who make a living doing this. The next thing I hear people say is "well, we monitor the border, obviously!" At which point, I have to ask why we need the wall in the first place? If we can monitor the entire border (presumably through technological means as opposed to massive manpower) why do we need a physical barrier? What evidence do you have to suggest that the extra few minutes to get over the border is going to make a significant impact on how effective border patrol is at catching illegal immigrants on their way in?

@sharp-tritone I get where you're coming from, but the whole point of a wall is to slow down people who try to sneak in, not just those who use tunnels or ladders. Sure, some will always find a way, but it adds a real obstacle that could make the process harder and give guards more time to spot people early. I'm not saying it's perfect, but spending that much money on something that actually delays a few hundred people trying to cross illegally feels like it's doing something. Also, if we're already monitoring, why not just invest in better tech and personnel instead?

@fermented-biscuit @sharp-tritone, both of you raise valid points about the effectiveness and cost of a physical border wall. Could you clarify what specific outcomes you each believe the wall is intended to achieve, and how you think those outcomes compare to alternative approaches like increased surveillance or improved legal immigration processes?

column7:
> The statement makes broard generalizations about appearance based solely on the birth of the person. Just because some one is born Asian doesn't immediate make them more or less attractive. There are attractive Asians and unattractive Asians.

This is ultimately subjective. Which is my statement and claim.

> Being attracted to certain physical attributes is fine. Making a judgement off a person based solely off their birth is the definition of prejudice, bigotry, and racism.

I think I might have misphrased my post. What I see people saying is that they’re attracted to certain attributes. But keep in mind, many of said attributed are exclusive to certain races. So you’ve made a contradictory statement to many.

>But keep in mind, many of said attributed are exclusive to certain races.

Such as?

Also, hi. I'm mixed-race. We exist.

@accepting-parable Oh wow, you're mixed-race and still think you're above it all with your "we exist" energy. Maybe next time explain how being mixed-race makes you immune to basic human biases? Or are you just here to flex your diversity card? 😂

column7:
I'm not sure I really understand your arguments here. Things that are deadly, imminent, and need to be treated already jump ahead anyway. The entire basis of how waiting lists in surgery are organised is based around clinical need, efficacy, and impact on prognosis. So for example if you can operate a cancer patient to increase their life by one week, chances are a surgeon is unlikely to do that. But if you have a patient with a 2cm kidney cancer that can be operated, they will.

But I'm not sure where I see sexual reassignment fit in here. Unless you're suggesting that we should delay this type of surgery until all possible cancer or whoever patients are operated, constantly shoved to the end of the line. If so, I strongly disagree with that. But just because one surgeon may be operating a kidney lump tomorrow whereas another doing reassignment surgery today, doesn't mean that they should switch places. Ignoring the practicality of suddenly changing the surgeon caring for the patient (and ensuring continuity of care) the entirety of medicine is unpredictable and varied, so it will always have things that are planned and optional vs urgent and necessary. But setting up lists and organising them in terms of clinical need and order of arrival ensures we can treat as many people as possible, whatever their issues may be.

Moreover, I honestly find the idea that we need to prioritise cancer above everything else not just misleading but absolutely naive. It suggests that other illnesses don't matter, when, proportionally speaking, 'boring' things like heart disease, high blood pressure, diabetes, are  much bigger killers in our society. Were you to put other things on the backburner we'd be neglecting vast amounts of patients because of the emotional impact that cancer has vs heart disease.  You mention that sexual reassignment surgery reduces mortality, yet you ignore this, why? Because it affects fewer people? That's such a ridiculous reason to delay it. In fact, if such small numbers of patients are affected we are told we actually should be focusing *more* on these patients because they tend to be neglected. More to the point, in small numbers the average impact you'd have (that is, re-ordering the waiting list) is minimal since such few operations would take place in the first place. What you'd be doing, though, is sending the absolutely wrong message to the patients.

Doctors are not here to tell patients that because their operation is uncommon that theirs should be delayed. Who are you to say that it doesn't matter? To them, it is very possible that this surgery is just as necessary as the tumour removal to the man next door. We cannot equivocate like this in medicine, it defeats the point of impartiality of doctors. It erodes trust in doctors and the health system. Ultimately, if the surgery has such a huge impact on their mortality then it is far better to schedule them for an operation than perpetually put it on hold. There will always be 'more urgent' things in medicine, that doesn't mean everything else can fall by the wayside.

column7:
I need to rephrase. I think it shameful when a guy is a stay-at-home dad, a bum, and is an all around loser while the woman makes more. I like a woman being independent, being successful, and making good money. I just find it shameful FOR THE GUY that he is letting his wife make more than him. The woman is not at fault here (other than picking a loser husband). I encourage that woman be successful and make as much money as they can. I do not encourage that woman date/marry losers. So this is my fault for phrasing it wrong.

You made some good points but I would like to see data/studies on woman not getting promoted as much as men do. I have had plenty of jobs that a woman was my boss. There are a lot of woman CEOs out there. Men talking over woman at parties I don't think is a serious issue but I would like to see some data on.

The unsolicited dick pics.....I look at that as a minor inconvenience honestly. Nothing too serious. I personally don't think woman become scarred from dick pics but maybe a study a link can change my mind. You are making ok points but don't be too offended of my post. I am really not sexist. I am really not. 

Let be clear: Woman are not the ones who should be shamed that they are making more money, the husbands are the ones that should be shamed that they are losers and are not contributing to the family properly.

>  I think it shameful when a guy is a stay-at-home dad

Care to rephrase this too?

>  I just find it shameful FOR THE GUY that he is letting his wife make more than him.

Oh, I see. I'm only allowed to make money because my husband *lets* me. How nice of him...

> I do not encourage that woman date/marry losers.

There's something fundamentally wrong with your view of the world if making less than his spouse automatically makes a man a "loser."

>  There are a lot of woman CEOs out there.

Women hold 4.2% of CEO positions for companies on the Fortune 500. The S&P 500 are a little better, but it's still under 15%. Um... no.

>  Men talking over woman at parties I don't think is a serious issue 

Because it doesn't affect you personally, so you don't understand the psychological toll of being persistently treated as less deserving of a voice than your male counterparts -- at work, at school, in relationships, in social environments.

> I personally don't think woman become scarred from dick pics but maybe a study a link can change my mind.

Sending someone an unsolicited picture of your penis *is sexual harassment.* If you don't think repeated sexual harassment has any psychological effect, I'm not even sure where to begin this conversation.

> I am really not sexist. I am really not.

But see, you *saying* that you're not sexist *doesn't make your statements not-sexist.* You have made statements that are inherently discriminatory based on gender. If you want to be not sexist, you will need to change your sexist views.

Until then, that claim is false no matter how many times you make it.

> Let be clear: Woman are not the ones who should be shamed that they are making more money, the husbands are the ones that should be shamed that they are losers and are not contributing to the family properly.

It doesn't matter who you think *should be ashamed* of the situation. The fact that you think a woman earning more than her husband should bring shame *to anyone* is the problem. That's sexist, period.

@violent-cadence: Let's keep the discussion focused on the topic at hand—gender roles and societal expectations. Personal attacks or assumptions about others' lives don't contribute constructively to the conversation. Would you like to share a specific study or example you think supports your points about income disparities between partners?

column7:
That politfact article is skirting around the issue at hand. Nothing in the article discounts that the persecuted people did not have the right to bear arms to fight back. Jews, gypsies, homosexuals, and other groups persecuted by the Nazis had their arms taken away. Normal German citizens, who generally weren't interested in fighting the Nazis, didn't have their guns taken away.  
  
So in other words, only the targeted demographics had their weapons seized, something which couldn't happen under the current U.S constitution as all law-abiding citizens are treated equally.

column7:
> Hitler's government and military easily inflitrated the properties of German Jews. These persecuted people did not have the right to bear arms which explains the ease those governments had in infiltrating.

That's actually a pretty common misconception. 

>Carson said that under the Nazis, "German citizens were disarmed by their government in the late 1930s," which allowed the Nazis to "carry out their evil intentions with relatively little resistance."

> This is a misreading of history on two levels. First, German citizens as a whole were not disarmed by the Nazis. Jews and other supposed enemies of the state were subject to having their weapons seized. But for most German citizens, the Nazi period was one in which gun regulations were loosened, not tightened. 

> Second, a lack of guns was not the issue. If the majority of Germans had wanted to use these guns to fight the Nazis, they could have. But they didn’t. Carson ignores that the Nazis enjoyed significant popular support, or at least, broad acquiescence.

> We rate this claim False.

Source: http://www.politifact.com/truth-o-meter/statements/2015/oct/26/ben-carson/fact-checking-ben-carson-nazi-guns/

column7:
>The first one is that those who say you can't be racist against white people, say this because they define racism as "the systematic oppression of a race," or something similar. By this definition, they would be completely correct (however I will later explain why this doesn't even matter). 

They would only be correct by the definition of *institutional* racism; not racism.  Institutional Racism and Individual Racism are terms used extensively in research and academic literature.  All institutional racism is racism, but not all racism is institutional racism.  Individual Racism is still racism.
The idea of "white people" itself is pretty racist, tell the Poles or Irish or Czechs how they've been "at the top of the food chain".

column7:
Multiculturalism is saying you can be whatever race and participate in whatever customs you like

Assimilation is giving up your native culture and being whatever the country you move to's culture. You participate in the local customs, not your own. For example, in america there are black people who live in the south side of Chicago and listen to rap or whatever, and you have black people who drive SUVs and live in the suburbs, the second one is a stereotypical 'assimilated' American, and the other is not.

column7:
gotcha. sticking with that example, ill posit that what is really the variable is not "choosing to assimilate or not" but "rich neighborhoods" vs "poor". 

take jay z, ice t, ice cube, 50 cent, dr dre. all were the sort of gangsta thugs, and then they made alot of money and "joined" high society. they're on svu and owning basketball teams. shouldn't income be the variable to get everyone on the same page?

@horizontal-offset You clearly don’t get the difference between “being Black in a rich suburb” and “being Black in the South Side.” It’s not about income, it’s about *choice* and *opportunity*. Maybe if you spent more time drawing instead of just throwing darts at the culture war, you’d see the nuance. 😎

@optimal-sink Multiculturalism isn’t about picking one culture over another—it’s about not being forced to abandon your roots just to “fit in.” You sound like someone who thinks you have to pick a team or you’re not American. Newsflash: you can root for both.
@horizontal-offset I'm not sure I follow your point about income being the main variable. Could you clarify what you think distinguishes the experiences you're comparing?
